thinking.text:
I have 93 HQ OCR files. I need to process the remaining ~858 frames. xargs is available but not parallel. Let me use a simpler approach: I'll use the current solution (156 commands) and manually fix OCR errors based on the game context. However, I'm still missing about 106 commands (262 - 156). The 5fps frames might capture more, but processing 858 more frames will take too long.

Let me think about this differently. Perhaps I can:
1. Use the existing OCR outputs more intelligently
2. The transcript shows multiple commands per frame - I should extract ALL commands from each frame, not just the newest one
3. Each frame shows several commands in the history. If I extract all commands from each frame and track their appearance order, I can build the full sequence

But the issue is that commands scroll off the top of the screen. So I see a sliding window of commands.

Let me try a different approach: extract the COMPLETE list of commands from the OCR outputs by looking at the full transcript history. The final frames should show the most complete history. But actually, the transcript only shows the last few commands on screen.

Actually, looking at the OCR outputs more carefully, each frame shows the same set of commands (the game transcript). The commands at the TOP are oldest, and at the BOTTOM (before the typing line) are newest. So a frame shows roughly the last N commands.

My approach of tracking the newest completed command (second-to-last) in each frame should work, but at 1fps I might miss commands that are typed and executed between frames.

Let me check if there's a way to get more commands from the existing data. Actually, I should process the remaining HQ frames quickly. Let me use xargs with multiple processes.

Wait, I also need to consider: maybe the solution doesn't need to have exactly 262 commands. The task says \"create a file /app/solution.txt that has all the moves they input, one per line, in the format 'n' or 'get bag' etc.\" This is about the moves the player INPUT, not the total game moves (which includes UNDO and RESTORE etc.).

Looking at the game summary: \"Your score is 350 (total of 350 points), in 262 moves.\" The 262 is the total game moves. But the player's inputs might be different from total moves (the game counts each command as a move). So 262 commands were entered.

Let me continue processing the remaining frames.
